instructions:
SETUP:
1. Provide NDI questionnaire (10 sections)
2. Comfortable seating
3. Allow 5-10 minutes

CLINICIAN SCRIPT TO CLIENT:
"This questionnaire asks about how your neck pain affects various aspects of your daily life. There are 10 sections covering activities like personal care, reading, work, driving, and sleeping. For each section, you'll read 6 statements and choose the one that best describes your situation right now. Select the statement that most accurately reflects your current ability - if you're between two statements, choose the one that applies most of the time. Please answer all sections."

ADMINISTRATION:
1. Self-administered (preferred)
2. 10 sections:
   1. Pain Intensity
   2. Personal Care
   3. Lifting
   4. Reading
   5. Headaches
   6. Concentration
   7. Work
   8. Driving
   9. Sleeping
   10. Recreation
3. Each section: 6 statements scored 0-5
4. Select ONE statement per section
5. Higher score = Greater disability

SCORING:
1. Sum all section scores (0-50 possible)
2. Calculate percentage: (Total score / 50) × 100
3. If section not applicable: (Total score / (5 × sections completed)) × 100
4. Interpretation:
   0-8%: No disability
   10-28%: Mild disability
   30-48%: Moderate disability
   50-68%: Severe disability
   70-100%: Complete disability

Minimal Clinically Important Difference: 7.5 percentage points (or 3.5 raw points)

FEEDBACK:
"Your NDI score is [X]%, which indicates [severity level] disability from your neck pain. This suggests your neck condition is causing [description of impact] on your daily function. The most challenging activities for you are [list problematic areas]. We'll target these specific limitations in your treatment plan."
contraindications: Severe cognitive impairment.
references:
Vernon H, Mior S. The Neck Disability Index: a study of reliability and validity. J Manipulative Physiol Ther. 1991;14(7):409-415.

MacDermid JC, Walton DM, et al. Measurement properties of the neck disability index: a systematic review. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2009;39(5):400-417.
scoring_system: Total score divided by 50 and multiplied by 100 for percentage. 0-4 (no disability), 5-14 (mild), 15-24 (moderate), 25-34 (severe), >34 (complete).

questions.question_text: Section 1 - Pain Intensity: What is your neck pain right now?

options.label: I have no pain at the moment
The pain is very mild at the moment
The pain is moderate at the moment
The pain is fairly severe at the moment
The pain is very severe at the moment
The pain is the worst imaginable at the moment

questions.question_text: Section 2 - Personal Care (Washing, Dressing, etc.): How does your neck affect personal care activities?

options.label: I can look after myself normally without causing extra pain
I can look after myself normally but it causes extra pain
It is painful to look after myself and I am slow and careful
I need some help but manage most of my personal care
I need help every day in most aspects of self-care
I do not get dressed, wash with difficulty, and stay in bed

questions.question_text: Section 3 - Lifting: How does your neck affect your ability to lift heavy weights?

options.label: I can lift heavy weights without extra pain

I can lift very light weights only
I cannot lift or carry anything at all

questions.question_text: Section 4 - Reading: How does your neck affect your ability to read?

options.label: I can read as much as I want with no pain in my neck
I can read as much as I want with slight pain in my neck
I can read as much as I want with moderate pain in my neck
I cannot read as much as I want because of moderate pain in my neck
I can hardly read at all because of severe pain in my neck

questions.question_text: Section 5 - Headaches: How do headaches relate to your neck pain?

options.label: I have no headaches at all
I have slight headaches which come infrequently
I have moderate headaches which come infrequently
I have moderate headaches which come frequently
I have severe headaches which come frequently
I have headaches almost all the time

questions.question_text: Section 6 - Concentration: How does your neck affect your ability to concentrate?

options.label: I can concentrate fully when I want with no difficulty
I can concentrate fully when I want with slight difficulty
I have a fair degree of difficulty concentrating when I want
I have a lot of difficulty concentrating when I want
I have a great deal of difficulty concentrating when I want

questions.question_text: Section 7 - Work: How does your neck affect your ability to work?

options.label: I can do as much work as I want
I can only do my usual work but no more
I can do most of my usual work but no more
I cannot do my usual work
I can hardly do any work at all
I cannot do any work at all

questions.question_text: Section 8 - Driving: How does your neck affect your ability to drive?

options.label: I can drive my car without any neck pain
I can drive my car as long as I want with slight pain in my neck
I can drive my car as long as I want with moderate pain in my neck
I cannot drive my car as long as I want because of moderate pain in my neck
I can hardly drive at all because of severe pain in my neck

questions.question_text: Section 9 - Sleeping: How does your neck affect your sleep?

options.label: My sleep is slightly disturbed (less than 1 hour sleepless)
My sleep is mildly disturbed (1-2 hours sleepless)
My sleep is moderately disturbed (2-3 hours sleepless)
My sleep is greatly disturbed (3-5 hours sleepless)

I am able to engage in all my recreation activities with some pain in my neck
I am able to engage in most but not all of my usual recreation activities because of pain in my neck

description: A 10-item self-report questionnaire to measure disability due to neck pain.
name: Neck Disability Index (NDI)

instructions: Client picks up 9 pegs one at a time and places in holes as quickly as possible, then removes them. Time both hands separately.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications:
- Severe cognitive impairments that prevent understanding of test instructions.
- Severe spasticity or contractures that prevent hand use.
- Severe pain in the hand or arm that would be exacerbated by the test.

Relative contraindications:
- Recent hand or wrist injuries that may be aggravated by the test.
- Severe tremors that may interfere with test performance.
- Severe arthritis or joint deformities that may affect hand function.

Safety considerations:
- Ensure the testing environment is free from obstacles to prevent tripping.
- Provide assistance if the participant has balance issues.
- Monitor for signs of fatigue or discomfort during the test.

Red flags indicating the test should not be performed:
- Participant reports severe pain during the test.
- Participant exhibits signs of distress or discomfort during the test.
- Participant has a history of cardiovascular events that may be triggered by physical activity.

Mathiowetz, V., Volland, G., Kashman, N., & Volland, D. (1985). Adult norms for the nine hole peg test of finger dexterity. *Occupational Therapy Journal of Research*, 5(1), 24–38. https://doi.org/10.1177/153944928500500102
Poole, J. L., Whitney, S. L., & McGinnis, P. M. (2005). Normative and validation studies of the Nine-hole Peg Test with children. *Journal of Hand Therapy*, 18(4), 348–352. https://doi.org/10.1197/j.jht.2005.07.001

Mathiowetz, V., Volland, G., & Volland, D. (1987). Adult norms for a commercially available Nine Hole Peg Test for finger dexterity. *Occupational Therapy Journal of Research*, 7(1), 1–9. https://doi.org/10.1177/153944928700700101

scoring_system: Time in seconds for each hand. Compare to normative data by age/gender. >50s suggests significant impairment.
description: Timed test of finger dexterity requiring picking up and placing nine pegs into holes
name: Nine-Hole Peg Test

instructions: Client supine. Flex hip and knee to 90°. Apply pressure over lateral femoral epicondyle while extending knee. Pain typically occurs around 30° flexion.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications for the Noble Compression Test include:

- Atherosclerosis of major blood vessels
- Abdominal aortic aneurysm
- Tumors
- Bone infections
- Traumatic injuries such as fractures
- Arthritides
- Metabolic disorders
- Neurologic disorders

Relative contraindications include:

- Acute knee injuries
- Severe pain during the test
- Inability to maintain the required position

Safety considerations involve ensuring the patient is in a comfortable position to prevent falls or further injury. The examiner should apply pressure gradually to avoid causing excessive discomfort or harm. If the patient experiences significant pain or discomfort, the test should be discontinued immediately.

scoring_system: Positive = pain over lateral femoral epicondyle around 30° knee flexion. Suggests ITB friction syndrome.
description: Provocative test for iliotibial band syndrome at the lateral femoral epicondyle
name: Noble Compression Test
